subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
fact_pattern: The Crown has obtained a signed statement from a witness who says the accused was definitely not at the location where the crime was committed. Crown has decided not to call this witness at trial and has not provided the statement to defence.
call_of_question: Is Crown required to disclose this exculpatory witness statement to defence?

options.A: No — the Crown need only disclose evidence it intends to introduce at trial.
options.B: Yes — under R v Stinchcombe, the Crown must disclose all relevant information regardless of whether it intends to use it at trial, including exculpatory evidence that may assist the defence.
options.C: Only if defence specifically requests the statement in a disclosure application.
options.D: No — a witness statement favourable to the accused is protected by litigation privilege.

explanation: R v Stinchcombe [1991] 3 SCR 326 established the foundational rule that the Crown must disclose all relevant information in its possession — whether inculpatory or exculpatory — to the defence. The obligation is not limited to evidence Crown intends to use at trial. An exculpatory alibi witness statement is precisely the kind of evidence that must be disclosed because it may assist the accused in making a full answer and defence, as guaranteed by s. 7 of the Charter.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe explicitly rejected limiting disclosure to evidence Crown plans to use. The duty extends to all relevant information, regardless of Crown's intended use.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — under R v Stinchcombe, the Crown must disclose all relevant information regardless of whether it intends to use it at trial, including exculpatory evidence that may assist the defence.
why_C_wrong: Stinchcombe imposes an affirmative and ongoing duty on Crown to disclose relevant material. Defence need not make a specific application for each item; the duty is proactive, not demand-driven.
why_D_wrong: Litigation privilege does not shield exculpatory evidence from Stinchcombe disclosure. The duty to make full answer and defence overrides any such claim in the context of relevant evidence.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
fact_pattern: An accused is charged with aggravated assault. Crown provides a package of initial disclosure two weeks before the scheduled election date. Defence counsel argues that disclosure should have been provided earlier and challenges the timing.
call_of_question: When must Stinchcombe disclosure be made to the accused?

options.A: Disclosure must be provided at the first court appearance.
options.B: Disclosure must be made before the accused is required to elect or enter a plea, and the duty is continuing as new information becomes available.
options.C: Disclosure must be provided at least 60 days before the trial date.
options.D: Crown may wait until the eve of trial to provide disclosure of all but the most critical evidence.

explanation: Stinchcombe held that initial disclosure must be made at the first reasonable opportunity and in any event before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial or enter a plea — because those decisions should be made with knowledge of the evidence. The duty is also explicitly continuing: as new information comes into Crown's possession, it must be disclosed promptly. Providing disclosure two weeks before the election date may be compliant depending on when the election is scheduled, but the core rule is that it must precede election and plea.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe does not require disclosure at the first appearance, although prompt disclosure is encouraged. The binding requirement is that disclosure precede election or plea.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Disclosure must be made before the accused is required to elect or enter a plea, and the duty is continuing as new information becomes available.
why_C_wrong: There is no fixed 60-day rule in Stinchcombe. The trigger is the election and plea, not a calendar deadline counted from trial.
why_D_wrong: Waiting until the eve of trial violates the Stinchcombe rule that disclosure must precede election and plea, and also violates the principle that the duty is continuing and timely.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
fact_pattern: Police used a confidential informant to gather evidence leading to the accused's arrest for drug trafficking. The informant's tip was central to obtaining the search warrant. Defence brings a Stinchcombe application seeking full disclosure of all information about the informant, including identity.
call_of_question: How does informer privilege interact with Crown's Stinchcombe duty to disclose?

options.A: Stinchcombe always prevails; the Crown must disclose all relevant information including the identity of any confidential informant.
options.B: Informer privilege is a near-absolute common law privilege; the identity of a confidential informant need not be disclosed even if such information is relevant, because the privilege protects informants and the public interest in law enforcement.
options.C: Informant identity is protected for 30 days following the relevant proceedings; after that period, Stinchcombe requires full disclosure.
options.D: Defence may always obtain informant identity disclosure by successfully bringing a Stinchcombe application.

explanation: Informer privilege is one of the recognised exceptions to the Stinchcombe duty to disclose. It is described as a near-absolute privilege at common law, designed to protect the safety of confidential informants and to preserve the public interest in the free flow of information to law enforcement. The Crown is not required to disclose the identity of a confidential informant even where that information is relevant and potentially helpful to the defence. The privilege can be displaced only in the rarest circumstances — notably, the innocence-at-stake exception.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe itself recognised that the disclosure duty is subject to exceptions, including privilege. Informer privilege is one such exception and can override the general disclosure rule.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Informer privilege is a near-absolute common law privilege; the identity of a confidential informant need not be disclosed even if such information is relevant, because the privilege protects informants and the public interest in law enforcement.
why_C_wrong: There is no 30-day time limit on informer privilege. The privilege persists indefinitely as a matter of law and does not expire after any fixed period.
why_D_wrong: A Stinchcombe application does not override informer privilege. Informant identity remains protected unless the accused can establish the innocence-at-stake exception, which is an extremely high threshold.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
fact_pattern: Crown complied fully with its initial Stinchcombe disclosure obligation. Two months before trial, the police laboratory completes a DNA analysis that was not yet available at the time of initial disclosure. The DNA results actually exculpate the accused. Crown does not provide this new report to defence until the morning of the first day of trial.
call_of_question: Has Crown violated its disclosure obligations by waiting until the day of trial to disclose the new DNA evidence?

options.A: No — once initial disclosure is complete, Crown has discharged its obligation and has no further duty to disclose.
options.B: No — evidence obtained after the initial disclosure is exempt from the continuing duty under Stinchcombe.
options.C: Yes — R v Stinchcombe imposes a continuing duty to disclose; the Crown must disclose new relevant evidence promptly as it is obtained, and waiting until the day of trial to disclose exculpatory DNA evidence may prejudice the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
options.D: No — Crown has a discretion to time the disclosure of forensic reports and may choose to disclose on the first day of trial.

explanation: Stinchcombe held that the disclosure duty is explicitly continuing — it does not end with the initial disclosure package. As new relevant information becomes available, it must be disclosed in a timely manner. Waiting two months to disclose exculpatory DNA evidence until the morning of trial is a breach of the continuing duty. Such late disclosure may deprive defence of the opportunity to properly investigate, reconsider the accused's election and plea, or seek a stay of proceedings. Crown has no discretion to strategically time the disclosure of relevant evidence.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe is explicit that the disclosure obligation continues throughout the proceedings. There is no exhaustion of duty upon completing initial disclosure.
why_B_wrong: There is no exemption for evidence obtained after initial disclosure. The continuing duty applies to all relevant information, regardless of when it comes into Crown's hands.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — R v Stinchcombe imposes a continuing duty to disclose; the Crown must disclose new relevant evidence promptly as it is obtained, and waiting until the day of trial to disclose exculpatory DNA evidence may prejudice the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
why_D_wrong: Crown has no discretion to strategically delay disclosure of relevant evidence. The duty is to disclose promptly. Deliberately timing disclosure to reduce defence preparation time would compound the breach.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
fact_pattern: During trial, Crown discloses for the first time a key police officer's notebook containing notes that undermine the officer's credibility. Defence had been unaware of the notes. Defence seeks a stay of proceedings on the basis of Crown's non-disclosure. The trial judge notes that the notes can be reviewed overnight and trial can resume the next day.
call_of_question: What is the appropriate remedy for Crown's non-disclosure, and in what circumstances will a stay of proceedings be granted?

options.A: A stay of proceedings is the automatic and mandatory remedy for any breach of Crown's Stinchcombe disclosure obligations.
options.B: The accused can never obtain a stay for non-disclosure; the only remedy is an adjournment.
options.C: The primary remedy is an adjournment to allow the accused to review the disclosed material; a stay of proceedings is reserved for cases where the non-disclosure has caused irreparable prejudice to the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
options.D: Crown is immune from any sanction for non-disclosure where the omission was inadvertent rather than deliberate.

explanation: The courts have established a spectrum of remedies for Crown non-disclosure. An adjournment to review newly disclosed material is the most common and least drastic remedy, appropriate where the prejudice can be cured by additional time. A stay of proceedings is the most serious Charter remedy and is reserved for cases where the accused has suffered irreparable prejudice to the right to make full answer and defence — prejudice that cannot be cured by any lesser remedy. On these facts, an overnight adjournment appears to cure the prejudice, making a stay inappropriate.
why_A_wrong: A stay is not automatic. It is the most exceptional remedy available and requires a finding of irreparable prejudice that cannot be remedied by less drastic measures.
why_B_wrong: A stay is available in extreme cases of non-disclosure where the prejudice is genuinely irreparable. It is not categorically unavailable — it is simply reserved for the most serious situations.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The primary remedy is an adjournment to allow the accused to review the disclosed material; a stay of proceedings is reserved for cases where the non-disclosure has caused irreparable prejudice to the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
why_D_wrong: The availability of a Charter remedy for non-disclosure does not depend on Crown's intent. Inadvertent non-disclosure can still result in an adjournment or, in extreme cases, a stay, depending on the prejudice caused.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
fact_pattern: Dominique is charged with sexual assault. Her defence counsel believes that the complainant's psychiatric records, which are held by a therapist and not by the Crown, may contain statements relevant to the complainant's credibility. Defence applies for production of those records to the court.
call_of_question: Which legal regime governs defence access to the complainant's psychiatric records in a sexual assault case?

options.A: Stinchcombe — because all relevant third party records must be disclosed by the Crown to the defence.
options.B: The O'Connor procedure — because all third party records in criminal proceedings are governed by R v O'Connor regardless of the nature of the offence.
options.C: Sections 278.1 to 278.91 of the Criminal Code (the Mills regime) — because these provisions specifically govern production of records relating to complainants in sexual offence proceedings and impose a stringent two-stage process.
options.D: The civil rules of evidence — because the records are held by a private third party and not by the Crown.

explanation: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91, Parliament enacted a specific statutory regime — often called the Mills regime — to govern production of records held by third parties relating to complainants in sexual offence proceedings. This regime was upheld in R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668. The accused must establish at stage one that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness, and must show the records are sought for a proper purpose (not relying on rape myths). The regime is more stringent than O'Connor and applies exclusively to complainant records in sexual offence cases.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe governs the Crown's duty to disclose materials in its possession. It does not govern applications for production of third party records held by private parties such as therapists.
why_B_wrong: The O'Connor procedure governs third party records in non-sexual offence cases. Where the complainant records relate to a sexual offence, ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code displace the O'Connor procedure.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Sections 278.1 to 278.91 of the Criminal Code (the Mills regime) — because these provisions specifically govern production of records relating to complainants in sexual offence proceedings and impose a stringent two-stage process.
why_D_wrong: Criminal proceedings are governed by criminal law rules, not civil evidence rules. The Criminal Code specifically addresses third party record production through the Mills regime and O'Connor, not civil discovery rules.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91; R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668

tested_concepts: Mills regime for sexual offence complainant records
Stinchcombe does not govern third party records

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
fact_pattern: Teodora is accused of assaulting her co-worker during a workplace dispute. Her defence counsel believes records from the complainant's employer — including internal investigation reports and disciplinary records — may reveal prior false complaints of workplace violence made by the complainant. These records are not in the Crown's possession.
call_of_question: What procedure must defence follow to obtain these workplace records, and what must be established?

options.A: Defence must bring a Stinchcombe application requiring the Crown to obtain the records through the police.
options.B: Defence must apply under ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code because those provisions apply to all third party records sought in criminal proceedings.
options.C: Defence must bring an application under the procedure established in R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, seeking production from the third party; the court will assess likely relevance and balance the accused's right to make full answer and defence against the privacy interests of the complainant.
options.D: Defence is not entitled to any third party records held by private employers unless the Crown first discloses them.

explanation: The two-stage procedure from R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, governs production of third party records in non-sexual offence proceedings. Under O'Connor, at the first stage the accused must satisfy the court that the records are likely relevant. If this threshold is met, the records are produced to the judge for review. At the second stage, the judge balances the accused's s. 7 right to make full answer and defence against privacy rights and other competing interests, and determines what, if any, portion should be produced to the accused. The ss. 278.1–278.91 regime does not apply here because this is not a sexual offence case.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe only governs materials the Crown already has or can reasonably obtain through the police investigation. Third party records held by an employer require a separate O'Connor application, not a Crown disclosure order.
why_B_wrong: Sections 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code apply exclusively to records relating to a complainant in a sexual offence proceeding. An assault charge in a workplace dispute is not a sexual offence, and that regime does not govern.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Defence must bring an application under the procedure established in R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, seeking production from the third party; the court will assess likely relevance and balance the accused's right to make full answer and defence against the privacy interests of the complainant.
why_D_wrong: Defence may apply directly for third party records through the O'Connor procedure without requiring Crown to first disclose them. The Crown's failure to possess records does not preclude the accused from seeking them from the holder.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46; R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411

tested_concepts: O'Connor applies to non-sexual offence third party records
privacy vs full answer and defence balance

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
fact_pattern: Henri is charged with sexual assault. His defence counsel brings an application seeking production of the complainant's personal diary, her medical records, and her employment records. Counsel argues that all three categories of records are 'relevant' to Henri's defence and should be produced under the O'Connor procedure.
call_of_question: Which statement best describes the correct legal analysis for this production application?

options.A: All three categories of records should be produced because they are all likely relevant to the sexual assault trial.
options.B: The O'Connor procedure governs all third party record applications in criminal cases, including sexual offence cases; the judge must order production of all three categories if they are likely relevant.
options.C: Because Henri is charged with a sexual offence, ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code govern the application for the diary, medical records, and employment records relating to the complainant; the accused must satisfy the more stringent statutory regime, not O'Connor, and cannot obtain production merely by showing likely relevance.
options.D: Because the records are relevant, Henri may obtain them automatically through the Crown's Stinchcombe disclosure obligations.

explanation: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.1, 'record' is defined broadly to include medical, psychiatric, therapeutic counselling, school, social work, employment, personal journals, and records of sexual conduct relating to the complainant. Where an accused is charged with a sexual offence and seeks production of records described in s. 278.1 that relate to the complainant, the stringent two-stage Mills regime under ss. 278.1–278.91 applies — not the O'Connor procedure. Henri's counsel's application under O'Connor is incorrect. Under the Mills regime, at stage one the accused must establish that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness and that production is sought for a proper purpose. Merely asserting general relevance, or relying on myths about sexual assault complainants, will not suffice.
why_A_wrong: Production of third party complainant records in sexual offence proceedings cannot be ordered on a bare showing of relevance. The ss. 278.1–278.91 regime imposes a more demanding threshold designed to protect complainant privacy and prevent reliance on rape myths.
why_B_wrong: O'Connor has been effectively displaced by ss. 278.1–278.91 for sexual offence complainant records. The Criminal Code regime governs those specific categories, and invoking O'Connor for such records is a legal error.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Because Henri is charged with a sexual offence, ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code govern the application for the diary, medical records, and employment records relating to the complainant; the accused must satisfy the more stringent statutory regime, not O'Connor, and cannot obtain production merely by showing likely relevance.
why_D_wrong: Stinchcombe governs the Crown's disclosure of materials in its possession. Third party records held by private individuals or institutions are not governed by Stinchcombe. The Crown's disclosure obligation does not extend to compelling third party record holders to produce records.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third Party Records — Production to the Defence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91; R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668; R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411

tested_concepts: Mills regime displaces O'Connor for sexual offence complainant records
relevance alone insufficient under Mills regime

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third Party Records — Two Stages of Mills Regime
fact_pattern: Ivan is charged with sexual assault. He applies under ss. 278.3–278.5 of the Criminal Code for production of the complainant's counselling records held by a social worker. At the first stage, the trial judge determines whether to order the records produced to the court. Ivan argues the records likely contain relevant information about the complainant's mental state at the time of the alleged assault.
call_of_question: Under ss. 278.4–278.5 of the Criminal Code (Mills regime), what must Ivan establish at Stage One of the two-stage production process?

options.A: Ivan must prove, on a balance of probabilities, that the records contain information relevant to his guilt or innocence.
options.B: Ivan must satisfy the judge that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness, and that production is necessary in the interests of justice.
options.C: Ivan must establish that the complainant consented to disclosure of the records to the court.
options.D: Ivan must prove that the records are available nowhere else and that the defence cannot obtain the same information through cross-examination.

explanation: Under s. 278.5(1) of the Criminal Code, at Stage One of the Mills regime, the judge shall order production of the record to the judge for review if satisfied that: (a) the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness; and (b) production of the record is necessary in the interests of justice. The 'likely relevant' standard is less stringent than balance of probabilities — it means there is a reasonable possibility the records contain relevant information. The accused cannot rely on assertions that fall within s. 278.3(4) (the enumerated impermissible bases, e.g., that the complainant has a history of making complaints).
why_A_wrong: The Stage One threshold under s. 278.5 is not 'likely relevant on a balance of probabilities.' The standard is whether there is a reasonable possibility — 'likely relevant' — not a balance of probabilities standard. The balance of probabilities language would set the bar too high.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. At Stage One under s. 278.5(1), the accused must satisfy the court that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to a witness's competence, and that production is necessary in the interests of justice.
why_C_wrong: Complainant consent is not a prerequisite for Stage One production to the court under the Mills regime. The whole point of the regime is to balance privacy and the accused's right to full answer and defence without requiring consent.
why_D_wrong: Section 278.5 does not require the accused to prove that no other source of the information exists or that the information cannot be obtained through cross-examination. While these factors may inform the court's balancing, they are not standalone prerequisites for Stage One production.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third Party Records — Mills Regime Stage One
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.5(1); R. v. Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Timing of Disclosure — Before Election and Plea
fact_pattern: Oliver is charged with break and enter. At his first appearance, the Crown provides him with a one-page synopsis rather than full disclosure. The Crown explains that the full disclosure package will be ready in three weeks, by which time Oliver will already have been required to make his election. Oliver's lawyer objects that the election should be postponed until full disclosure is provided.
call_of_question: Under the Stinchcombe duty to disclose, is defence counsel's objection legally sound?

options.A: No — the election must proceed on the scheduled date regardless of whether full disclosure has been provided; disclosure is a separate process.
options.B: Yes — Stinchcombe requires that initial disclosure be made before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial; requiring election before disclosure violates the right to make full answer and defence.
options.C: No — a synopsis is sufficient disclosure for the purpose of election; full production may follow later.
options.D: Yes — but only if the charge is a serious indictable offence; for minor offences the election may proceed without disclosure.

explanation: In R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326, the Supreme Court held that the duty to disclose must be complied with before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial or enter a plea. The rationale is that the accused cannot meaningfully decide whether to elect a jury trial, a judge-alone trial, or a preliminary inquiry — and cannot make a fully informed guilty plea — without knowing what evidence the Crown holds. Providing only a synopsis and requiring election before full disclosure violates the Stinchcombe obligation. The appropriate remedy would be an adjournment of the election until initial disclosure is complete.
why_A_wrong: Stinchcombe is explicit that disclosure must precede the election. Proceeding to election before disclosure would deprive the accused of the ability to make an informed decision about mode of trial, violating s. 7 of the Charter.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Stinchcombe requires initial disclosure before election or plea. Defence counsel's objection is legally sound and the election should be postponed until full disclosure is provided.
why_C_wrong: A synopsis is not equivalent to full initial disclosure for the purpose of satisfying the Stinchcombe timing requirement. A synopsis does not give the accused sufficient information to make an informed election or plea.
why_D_wrong: The Stinchcombe timing requirement — disclosure before election — applies to all criminal charges regardless of seriousness. There is no exception for minor indictable offences. The right to make full answer and defence applies across the board.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Timing of Disclosure — Before Election and Plea
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7

tested_concepts: Stinchcombe timing — before election and plea
s. 7 right to full answer and defence

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: At trial for robbery, mid-way through defence evidence, the Crown discloses for the first time a surveillance video that captures a person who closely resembles the accused near the crime scene at the relevant time. Defence counsel had no opportunity to investigate the video's provenance or to retain an expert. The video is highly probative for the Crown. Defence seeks a stay of proceedings on the basis of the Crown's non-disclosure.
call_of_question: What is the appropriate analytical framework for assessing whether a stay of proceedings should be granted for Crown non-disclosure of the surveillance video?

options.A: A stay should be granted automatically whenever non-disclosure occurs at trial because late disclosure is always irreparably prejudicial.
options.B: The court should exclude the video from evidence as the exclusive remedy for late disclosure; a stay is not available.
options.C: The court should first determine whether the accused's right to make full answer and defence has been prejudiced; if so, the court considers whether a lesser remedy (such as an adjournment or exclusion of the video) can adequately cure the prejudice; a stay is warranted only if no lesser remedy adequately addresses the prejudice.
options.D: A stay is automatically warranted whenever non-disclosure occurs during the defence case because that phase of trial cannot be repeated.

explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada has established that the remedy for Crown non-disclosure is contextual and proportionate. Where non-disclosure is established, the court must assess the prejudice to the accused's ability to make full answer and defence. Available remedies escalate in severity: an adjournment to allow review and investigation is typically the first and least drastic option; exclusion of the withheld evidence may be available in some circumstances; and a stay of proceedings is the most exceptional remedy, reserved for cases where the prejudice is irreparable and no lesser remedy adequately addresses the violation. A stay is not automatic merely because non-disclosure occurred; the court must work through the spectrum of remedies.
why_A_wrong: A stay is not automatic on late disclosure. The court must assess whether the prejudice is irreparable and whether no lesser remedy (such as an adjournment for investigation) can cure the harm. Automatic stays would be disproportionate where lesser remedies can address the prejudice.
why_B_wrong: Exclusion of the evidence is one available remedy but is not the exclusive remedy for non-disclosure. Depending on the circumstances, an adjournment or other measure may be more appropriate. The court exercises discretion over the choice of remedy.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The remedy analysis for non-disclosure involves assessing prejudice to full answer and defence, considering the spectrum of remedies, and granting a stay only where no lesser remedy adequately cures the prejudice.
why_D_wrong: A stay is not automatic because non-disclosure occurred during the defence phase of trial. The timing of the non-disclosure is a relevant factor in assessing prejudice, but it does not trigger automatic stay entitlement. The court must still assess whether the prejudice is irreparable.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Remedies for Non-Disclosure
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7, s. 24(1); R. v. O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411 (remedy framework)

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Constructive Possession of Disclosure — Crown's Duty Regarding Police Files
fact_pattern: During a robbery investigation, police collected witness statements, video footage, and forensic reports. Crown counsel reviews the file and discloses what is in the Crown brief but does not review the complete police investigation file. Some relevant witness statements in the police file are never disclosed to defence. Defence argues that the Crown's duty extends to these statements.
call_of_question: Under the Stinchcombe duty to disclose, does the Crown's disclosure obligation extend to materials in the police investigation file that were not placed in the Crown brief?

options.A: No — the Crown's duty is limited to materials in the Crown brief that the prosecutor personally reviewed.
options.B: No — the police and the Crown are separate entities; the Crown cannot be responsible for materials it never received.
options.C: Yes — the Crown is deemed to be in constructive possession of all materials gathered by the police in the investigation; the duty to disclose extends to all relevant material in the possession of police acting in a prosecutorial capacity.
options.D: Yes — but only if the accused can prove the police deliberately withheld the material from the Crown.

explanation: Stinchcombe established that the Crown's duty to disclose extends beyond materials physically in the prosecutor's file. The Crown is deemed to have constructive possession of all materials in the possession of the police who conducted the investigation because the police act as agents of the prosecutorial process. Accordingly, the Crown must inquire into and disclose relevant materials held by police even if those materials were never placed in the formal Crown brief. The failure to review and disclose relevant police investigation materials is a breach of the Stinchcombe obligation.
why_A_wrong: The Stinchcombe duty is not limited to materials the prosecutor personally reviewed or placed in the Crown brief. The duty extends to materials in the constructive possession of the Crown through the police acting in a prosecutorial role.
why_B_wrong: Although the police and Crown are legally distinct, for the purposes of disclosure the Crown is treated as having constructive possession of police investigation materials gathered in the course of the prosecution. The Crown cannot disclaim responsibility for relevant police files by treating them as a separate entity.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Stinchcombe, the Crown has constructive possession of materials in the police's possession gathered as part of the investigation, and the disclosure duty extends to those materials.
why_D_wrong: Deliberate withholding by police is not required before constructive possession attaches. The Crown's duty is to inquire into and disclose all relevant materials held by police, regardless of whether the non-disclosure was deliberate or inadvertent.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Constructive Possession — Police Investigation Files
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3

tested_concepts: Crown constructive possession of police files

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: At a robbery trial, defence counsel requests the complete personnel file of the investigating detective, including disciplinary records, performance reviews, and sick leave records from the past five years. Crown refuses, arguing the material is irrelevant and that Stinchcombe does not require disclosure of a police officer's entire employment history.
call_of_question: Under the Stinchcombe disclosure framework, what is the relevance threshold that determines whether material must be disclosed to the defence?

options.A: The Crown must disclose any material that might reasonably be used by the accused in making full answer and defence, which includes material that is potentially relevant.
options.B: The Crown must disclose only material it considers directly admissible at trial.
options.C: The Crown must disclose all material regardless of relevance; the defence determines what is useful.
options.D: The Crown must disclose material only if the accused can demonstrate on a balance of probabilities that it will assist the defence.

explanation: Stinchcombe established a broad relevance standard for disclosure: the Crown must disclose information that is not clearly irrelevant or clearly privileged. More specifically, any information that may reasonably be used by the accused in making full answer and defence must be disclosed. This includes information that is potentially useful to the defence even if not directly admissible. For police officer personnel files, R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3 established a specific framework: the Crown must disclose records of serious misconduct by a police witness if those records are relevant to issues at trial (including credibility). However, a blanket request for all employment records without demonstrated potential relevance may be refused. The relevance threshold is broad but not unlimited.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Stinchcombe, the relevance standard is whether material is potentially useful to the accused in making full answer and defence — not merely whether it is directly admissible at trial.
why_B_wrong: Direct admissibility is too narrow a standard. Stinchcombe requires disclosure of material that may be useful to the defence even if it is not directly admissible at trial — for example, information that could lead to admissible evidence or assist in cross-examination.
why_C_wrong: Stinchcombe does not require disclosure of all material regardless of relevance. The Crown may decline to disclose material that is clearly irrelevant or clearly privileged. The duty applies to relevant material, not every document in the Crown's possession.
why_D_wrong: The accused does not bear the burden of proving relevance on a balance of probabilities before disclosure is triggered. The standard is whether the material is potentially relevant — a broad standard assessed by the Crown (subject to challenge). The accused need not prove the value of the disclosure.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Stinchcombe — Relevance Threshold
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third Party Records — Impermissible Bases under Mills Regime
fact_pattern: Conrad is charged with sexual assault. In his s. 278.3 application, he argues that the complainant's therapy records should be produced because: (i) the complainant has a history of sexual activity with third parties; (ii) the complainant previously made a complaint of sexual assault against another person; and (iii) the therapy records may contain information about the complainant's perception and memory at the relevant time.
call_of_question: Under s. 278.3(4) of the Criminal Code, which of Conrad's stated grounds is an impermissible basis for the application?

options.A: Grounds (i) and (ii) are impermissible bases; ground (iii) is a permissible basis.
options.B: Ground (iii) is the only impermissible basis; grounds (i) and (ii) may support a production application.
options.C: All three grounds are impermissible under s. 278.3(4).
options.D: Ground (i) is permissible if the prior sexual activity involved the accused; grounds (ii) and (iii) are impermissible.

explanation: Section 278.3(4) of the Criminal Code lists specific assertions that are deemed insufficient, alone, to establish that a record is likely relevant. These include assertions that: (a) the record exists; (b) the record relates to medical or psychiatric treatment; (c) the record may reveal a pattern of conduct; (d) the record relates to the credibility of the complainant; (e) the accused needs the records to cross-examine the complainant; (f) the records may contain an admission; (g) the complainant has a history of making complaints; and (h) the complainant has previously been sexually active with the accused or others. Grounds (i) (prior sexual activity with third parties) and (ii) (history of making sexual assault complaints) are both expressly listed as impermissible bases under s. 278.3(4). Ground (iii) — that the records may contain information about the complainant's perception and memory at the relevant time — relates to a specific testimonial issue (the witness's perception and memory capacity) and is not listed as an impermissible basis.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 278.3(4) specifically lists prior sexual activity (ground i) and history of making complaints (ground ii) as impermissible standing-alone bases. Ground (iii) relates to perception and memory, which is not enumerated as an impermissible basis and may support a production application.
why_B_wrong: Ground (iii) is not impermissible under s. 278.3(4). Evidence about the complainant's perception and memory at the relevant time directly relates to testimonial capacity and reliability — a legitimate purpose not enumerated as an impermissible basis. It is grounds (i) and (ii) that are impermissible.
why_C_wrong: All three grounds are not impermissible. Ground (iii) — relating to perception and memory — is not listed in s. 278.3(4) as an impermissible basis and can legitimately support a production application.
why_D_wrong: Prior sexual activity of the complainant with the accused or any other person is an impermissible basis under s. 278.3(4)(h), regardless of whether the prior activity involved the accused. The accused cannot use prior sexual history — whether with himself or third parties — as a basis for a production application.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third Party Records — Impermissible Bases
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.3(4)
